content: $$A = \left[ {\matrix{ 1 & 2 \cr 0 & 1 \cr } } \right]$$ then by the principle of mathematical induction, prove that $${A^n} = \left[ {\matrix{ 1 & {2n} \cr 0 & 1 \cr } } \right]$$
explanation:
Let given statement is denoted by P(n)

So, $$P(n):{A^n} = \left[ {\matrix{ 1 & {2n} \cr 0 & 1 \cr } } \right]$$ ..... (i)

Putting n = 1

$$P(1):{A^1} = \left[ {\matrix{ 1 & {2.1} \cr 0 & 1 \cr } } \right] = \left[ {\matrix{ 1 & 2 \cr 0 & 1 \cr } } \right] = A$$ (given)

$$\therefore$$ P(n) is true for n = 1

Let P(n) is true for n = k

i.e. $$P(k):{A^k} = \left[ {\matrix{ 1 & k \cr 0 & 1 \cr } } \right]$$ ..... (ii)

Now, we prove for n = k + 1 i.e.

$$P(k + 1):{A^{k + 1}} = \left[ {\matrix{ 1 & {2(k + 1)} \cr 0 & 1 \cr } } \right]$$ ..... (iii)

$$\therefore$$ $${A^{k + 1}} = {A^k} \times {A^1} = \left[ {\matrix{ 1 & {2k} \cr 0 & 1 \cr } } \right] \times \left[ {\matrix{ 1 & 2 \cr 0 & 1 \cr } } \right]$$ (from (ii))

$$ = \left[ {\matrix{ {1 \times 1 + 2k \times 0} & {1 \times 2 + 2k \times 1} \cr {0 \times 1 + 1 \times 0} & {0 \times 2 + 1 \times 1} \cr } } \right]$$

$$ = \left[ {\matrix{ 1 & {2 + 2k} \cr 0 & 1 \cr } } \right] = \left[ {\matrix{ 1 & {2(k + 1)} \cr 0 & 1 \cr } } \right]$$

$$\therefore$$ $${A^{k + 1}} = \left[ {\matrix{ 1 & {2(k + 1)} \cr 0 & 1 \cr } } \right]$$

$$\therefore$$ P(n) is true for n = k + 1 $$\therefore$$ P(n) is true for all n $$\in$$ N

content: Prove by induction that for all n $$\in$$ N, n2 + n is an even integer (n $$\ge$$ 1).
explanation:
Let P(n) : n2 + n is an even integer be the mathematical statement

For n = 1, 12 + 1 = 2 is an even integer

$$\therefore$$ P(1) is true

Let P(r) be true i.e. r2 + r is an even integer.

Let r2 + r = 2k, where k $$\in$$ N

Now, $${(r + 1)^2} + (r + 1) = ({r^2} + r) + (2r + 2)$$

$$ = 2k + 2r + 2 = 2(k + r + 1)$$ is also an even integer

$$\therefore$$ P(r + 1) is also true, if P(r) was taken as true. Also, P(1) was true.

Hence, from principle of mathematical induction we can say that n2 + n is an even integer (n $$\ge$$ 1, n $$\in$$ N)

content: Product of any r consecutive natural numbers is always divisible by
explanation:
Product of r consecutive natural numbers

= 1 . 2 . 3 . 4 . ...... . r = r! which is divisible by r! .

content:
For each n $$\in$$ N, 23n $$-$$ 1 is divisible by

here N is a set of natural numbers.
explanation:
Let P(n) = 23n $$-$$ 1

Putting n = 1

P(1) = 23 . 1 $$-$$ 1 = 7 which is divisible by 7

Putting n = 2

P(2) = 23 . 2 $$-$$ 1 = 26 $$-$$ 1 = 63 which is divisible by 7 and so on

Let P(k) = 23k $$-$$ 1 is also divisible by 7

$$\therefore$$ 23k $$-$$ 1 = 7P $$\Rightarrow$$ 23k = 7P + 1

P(k + 1) = 23(k + 1) $$-$$ 1 = 23k . 23 $$-$$ 1

= (7P + 1)8 $$-$$ 1 = 7 . 8P + 8 $$-$$ 1 = 7(8P + 1)

$$\therefore$$ By process of M.I. P(k) is divisible by 7.

So, 23n $$-$$ 1 is divisible by 7.

content: The expression $2^{4 n}-15 n-1$, where $n \in \mathbb{N}$ (the set of natural numbers) is divisible by
explanation:
We need to determine which number $2^{4 n}-15 n-1$ is divisible by for $n \in N$.

Let $f(n)=2^{4 n}-15 n-1=16^n-15 n-1$.

Using the binomial expansion of $(1+15)^n$.

$$ \begin{aligned} & 16^n=(1+15)^n=\binom{n}{0}+\binom{n}{1} 15+\binom{n}{2} 15^2 \\ & +\binom{n}{3} 15^3+\ldots \\ & \Rightarrow 16^n=1+15 n+\frac{n(n-1)}{2} 225+\frac{n(n-1)(n-2)}{6} \\ & 3375+\ldots \end{aligned} $$

Substituting this into $f(n)$ :

$$ \begin{aligned} & f(n)=\left(1+15 n+\frac{n(n-1)}{2} 225+\ldots .\right)-15 n-1 \\ \Rightarrow & f(n)=\frac{n(n-1)}{2} 225+\frac{n(n-1)(n-2)}{6} 3375+\ldots \\ \Rightarrow & f(n)=15^2\left(\frac{n(n-1)}{2}+\frac{n(n-1)(n-2)}{6} \times 15+\ldots .\right) \end{aligned} $$

Since, all terms from $\binom{n}{2} 15^2$ onwards are multiples of $15^2=225, f(n)$ is divisible by 225.

options.content: P(n) is not divisible by any prime integer.
there exists prime integer which divides P(n).
P(n) is divisible by 5 for all $$n \in N$$.
P(n) is divisible by 3 for all $$n \in N$$.

explanation:
Given, $${P}(n)=3^{2 n+1}+2^{n+2}, n \in N$$

At $$n=1, P(1)=3^3+2^3=35$$

Which is divisible by $$(3+2)=5$$

i.e. a prime number.

We have,

72n + 16n $$-$$ 1

Put n = 1, we get

72 + 16 $$-$$ 1

= 49 + 15

= 64

which is divisible by 64.
